items.prompt_md: In the longest increasing subsequence dp table, what does dp[i] represent?
options: The length of the longest increasing subsequence that ends exactly at index i
The length of the longest increasing subsequence using only the first i elements
The number of increasing subsequences ending at index i
Whether nums[i] belongs to some increasing subsequence
items.answer: The length of the longest increasing subsequence that ends exactly at index i
items.explanation_md: dp[i] is scoped to subsequences ending at i specifically, which is why the final answer is max(dp) rather than dp[n-1].

items.prompt_md: You need the length of the longest subsequence that stays in increasing order, where elements may be skipped but must keep their original relative order. Which approach fits?
options: For each index i, take 1 + the best dp value among all earlier indices with a smaller value, then answer with the max over the whole table
Sort the array and count how many elements differ from their neighbor by more than 1
Use two pointers converging from both ends of the sorted array
Track a running max and min while making one linear pass
items.answer: For each index i, take 1 + the best dp value among all earlier indices with a smaller value, then answer with the max over the whole table
items.explanation_md: Sorting would destroy the order constraint the problem cares about; the dp table is what correctly weighs every valid earlier extension point.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the direct dp table for longest increasing subsequence, before applying the patience-sorting/binary-search optimization?
items.explanation_md: For each of n indices we scan all earlier indices, giving O(n^2) time and O(n) space; the optimized version reaches O(n log n) with a binary search over tail values.
